entries.title: Zoning Basics
entries.body:
**Zoning code has 2 parts: Map + Regulations**

**Map** assigns every parcel to a district:
- Residential (single family → high rise)
- Commercial (retail, restaurants, hotels)
- Industrial
- Institutional (schools, public buildings)
- Open Space

Districts separate uses AND intensity (e.g. C1, C2, C3 for increasing commercial density).

**Overlay Zones** layer on top of other zones adding additional restrictions/regulations — this is what the GNOD is.

**Regulations (zoning ordinance)** contain:
- Use tables — what's allowed, conditional, or prohibited per zone
- Development standards — setbacks, side yards, min lot sizes, building heights
- Impact regulations — signage, parking standards, historic preservation (not zone-specific)

entries.title: Missing Middle Housing
entries.body:
**The problem:** America built a two-option system — sprawl OR towers — by making everything in between illegal.

**Missing middle** = duplexes, triplexes, fourplexes, courtyard cottages, townhouses, small apartment buildings. Legal before ~1940s. Zoned out of existence after WWII via Euclidean zoning + car-centric policy.

**Why developers build towers instead:** Zoning approval is costly and uncertain. Only worth fighting if profit margin justifies it. Middle housing doesn't pencil out under current rules.

**Parking minimums** compound the problem — requiring X spaces per unit makes dense small buildings financially impossible.

**Examples of reform:**
- Minneapolis (2019): abolished single-family zoning, allows duplexes/triplexes citywide
- Portland: allows fourplexes and cottage courts
- Montgomery County MD: 50/50 market rate / affordable model

**Form-based codes** regulate size/shape of buildings instead of use — an alternative to traditional Euclidean zoning.

entries.title: Exclusionary Zoning
entries.body:
Zoning has historically been used for exclusion — originally explicitly by race/ethnicity, now through economic mechanisms.

**Modern exclusionary tools:**
- Large minimum lot sizes (1+ acre requirements → only expensive homes built)
- High parking minimums
- Setback/height restrictions that increase construction cost
- Single-family-only zoning over vast areas (San Jose: 94% of residential land)

**Effect:** Cities become enclaves for the wealthy. Working families, essential workers, and seniors get priced out.

**Segregation of uses** also creates sterile environments and forces car dependency — mixed-use zoning is the antidote but not widespread.
